concept_name: Writing a Vector in Unit-Vector (i-hat, j-hat, k-hat) Form
source_book: DC Pandey Chapter 5.3 — Resolution of Vectors; split from vector_components bundle

real_world_anchor.primary: Navigating Connaught Place in Delhi. To tell someone exactly where a shop is, you say 'three blocks east and two blocks north' — giving separate east and north movements. You rarely say 'walk 3.6 blocks at 34 degrees north of east', even though both describe the same location. The i-hat, j-hat form is physics's version of 'east-and-north' — it splits any vector into clean, independent axis-aligned pieces.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A drone delivery in Bengaluru. Its onboard computer tracks position as x-metres east plus y-metres north plus z-metres up. Each number is multiplied by its own unit vector (i-hat, j-hat, k-hat) to specify WHICH direction that number applies to. Without this notation, the computer cannot separate 'go east' from 'go up'.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: A cricket shot commentator says 'he played it late, fine, toward third man'. That is geometric. A physicist would say the ball's velocity is twelve i-hat plus three j-hat metres per second — sharply forward with a small lateral component. Both describe the same shot; the second is easier to compute with.

mag_check.formula: sqrt((A_mag * cos(theta_deg * PI / 180))*(A_mag * cos(theta_deg * PI / 180)) + (A_mag * sin(theta_deg * PI / 180))*(A_mag * sin(theta_deg * PI / 180)))

formulas.component_form_3d: A = Ax * i_hat + Ay * j_hat + Az * k_hat

constraints: i_hat, j_hat, k_hat are pure direction labels. Each has length 1 and no units
Ax and Ay carry the units of A (N, m, m/s, etc.)
Components reconstruct the magnitude by Pythagoras, NEVER by simple addition
Theta is measured from the +x axis; its convention must be stated

STATE_1.title: Why Split a Vector Into Parts?

scene_composition.text:
One arrow. One magnitude.
One angle.

Why split it into parts?

Because physics equations live
on specific axes. Force along x,
acceleration along y, velocity along z.

Splitting lets each axis be handled
independently — then sewn back together.

tts_sentences.text_en: You already know a vector as one arrow with a magnitude and a direction. So why complicate it by breaking it into pieces?
The reason is practical. Almost every physics equation lives on a specific axis. Newton's second law is applied along x and along y separately. Kinematics formulas work axis by axis.
If we keep a vector as a single tilted arrow, we cannot plug it into those equations directly. We must first break it into its axis-aligned parts.
The unit-vector form is the standard notation for this breakdown. The next three states build it step by step.

STATE_2.title: Meet the Unit Vectors — i-hat, j-hat, k-hat

scene_composition.text:
Three special arrows

i-hat points along +x
j-hat points along +y
k-hat points along +z

Each has length EXACTLY 1.
Each carries NO physical units
(they are pure direction labels).

Physics adopts them as its
standard reference directions.

tts_sentences.text_en: Physics fixes three reference arrows called unit vectors — i-hat, j-hat, and k-hat.
i-hat points along the positive x-axis. j-hat points along the positive y-axis. k-hat points along the positive z-axis, out of the page.
Each has length exactly one, and each carries no physical units. They are pure labels that say 'this way' without adding any newtons or metres of their own.
Think of them as compass points formalised. Every vector in physics is going to be written as numbers multiplied by these three hats.

STATE_3.title: Build the Vector — A = Ax i-hat + Ay j-hat

scene_composition.text:
Drop perpendiculars

Shadow of A on x-axis = A cos theta
  = 5 * 0.6 = 3
  That is Ax.

Shadow of A on y-axis = A sin theta
  = 5 * 0.8 = 4
  That is Ay.

Writing the vector

  A = Ax * i-hat + Ay * j-hat
    = 3 i-hat + 4 j-hat

One arrow. Two numbers. Two hats.
Captures everything about A.

tts_sentences.text_en: Now combine the two ideas. Take a tilted vector A of magnitude five at fifty-three degrees from the x-axis.
Drop a perpendicular from the tip of A onto the x-axis. The horizontal shadow has length A cosine theta, which is five times zero point six equals three. That is Ax.
Now drop a perpendicular onto the y-axis. The vertical shadow has length A sine theta, which is five times zero point eight equals four. That is Ay.
The vector is now written as A equals three i-hat plus four j-hat. Three units along x and four units along y.
The form is always 'number times i-hat plus number times j-hat'. The numbers carry the size. The hats carry the direction.

STATE_4.title: Worked Example — A Force of 25 N at 37 deg

scene_composition.text:
Given  F = 25 N, theta = 37 deg

Step 1  cos 37 = 0.8
         sin 37 = 0.6
Step 2  Fx = 25 * 0.8 = 20 N
         Fy = 25 * 0.6 = 15 N
Step 3  F = 20 i-hat + 15 j-hat  N

Magnitude check
  |F| = sqrt(400 + 225) = sqrt(625) = 25
  Original magnitude recovered.

Direction check
  tan theta = 15 / 20 = 0.75
  theta = 37 deg.  Correct.

tts_sentences.text_en: A force of twenty-five newtons acts at thirty-seven degrees above the horizontal.
Cosine thirty-seven is zero point eight. Sine thirty-seven is zero point six. The classic 3-4-5 triangle.
Fx is twenty-five times zero point eight, which is twenty newtons. Fy is twenty-five times zero point six, which is fifteen.
Write the force as twenty i-hat plus fifteen j-hat newtons. That is the full vector.

Always finish with two sanity checks. Take the root of the sum of squares — you should recover twenty-five. Take the inverse tangent of fifteen over twenty — you should recover thirty-seven degrees. Both match, so the decomposition is correct.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Magnitude of A equals Ax plus Ay

STATE_1.title: Your Belief — Ax + Ay Should Equal A

scene_composition.text:
Your reasoning

Ax = 3, Ay = 4
=> |A| = 3 + 4 = 7?

But the diagonal arrow
actually has length 5, not 7.

What went wrong?

tts_sentences.text_en: Students often add the two components to get the magnitude. Three plus four is seven, therefore the original vector has size seven.
That answer feels natural because for scalar quantities — mass, money — addition is how you combine things.
But Ax and Ay are not along the same direction. One points east, one points north. They cannot be added like scalars.

STATE_2.title: Follow the Arrows — You Walked a 3-4-5 Triangle

scene_composition.text:
Walk 3 east, then 4 north.
Total distance walked: 7 m.
End point: only 5 m from start.

The straight-line magnitude
obeys Pythagoras
|A|^2 = Ax^2 + Ay^2
     = 9 + 16 = 25
|A| = 5

NOT 7.

tts_sentences.text_en: Let us walk the path physically. Three metres east, then four metres north.
Total distance on your feet — seven metres. But straight-line distance from start to finish — only five metres.
The straight-line value is what the magnitude of A means. It obeys Pythagoras — magnitude squared equals Ax squared plus Ay squared.
Three squared plus four squared is twenty-five. Square root is five. That is the right answer.
Adding three and four to get seven would measure your walked path. Magnitude measures the diagonal.

STATE_3.title: The Formula You Will Always Use

scene_composition.text:
Magnitude from components

  |A| = sqrt(Ax^2 + Ay^2)

In 3D
  |A| = sqrt(Ax^2 + Ay^2 + Az^2)

Never Ax + Ay.
Never Ax - Ay.
Never just |Ax|.

Square each component.
Add the squares.
Take the root.

Sanity check

For any non-zero Ax and Ay,
  |A|  <  Ax + Ay

The magnitude is always
smaller than the simple sum.
If your answer is larger,
you made a mistake.

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the formula to memorise. Magnitude of A equals the square root of Ax squared plus Ay squared.
In three dimensions, add Az squared under the root. Everything else is unchanged.
There is a quick sanity check — the magnitude is always smaller than the sum of the absolute components. If you get a magnitude that exceeds the sum, you know you added instead of taking the root.
Square, add, root. Never just add.

regeneration_variants.label: Different magnitude, same decomposition
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A = 10 units at 53 deg -> Ax = 6, Ay = 8 (6-8-10 triangle). Same pattern as 3-4-5, scaled up.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Ax = |A| cos theta, Ay = |A| sin theta, |A| = sqrt(Ax^2 + Ay^2)

regeneration_variants.label: 3D decomposition
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Position vector r = 3 i-hat + 4 j-hat + 12 k-hat has magnitude sqrt(9 + 16 + 144) = 13. Classic 3-4-12-13 box diagonal.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: In 3D, three components give a box diagonal; formula extends by adding Az squared
